md: An **altitude** of a triangle is a perpendicular segment from a vertex to the opposite side (extended if necessary). Every triangle has three altitudes.
The three altitudes meet at a single point: the **orthocenter** $H$. Toggle it on below and drag a vertex around — three dashed purple lines, each perpendicular to a side, always concur at $H$.

blocks.title: Three places $H$ can hide
blocks.body:
**Acute triangle:** $H$ is *inside* the triangle.
**Right triangle:** $H$ is *at* the right-angle vertex itself.
**Obtuse triangle:** $H$ is *outside* the triangle.

The orthocenter has the widest range of any of the classical centers — try dragging a vertex to make an obtuse triangle and watch $H$ flee.

md: There are a few proofs. One slick way: through each vertex draw a line *parallel to the opposite side*. This creates a bigger triangle whose sides have the original triangle's vertices as midpoints. The altitudes of the small triangle become the **perpendicular bisectors of the big triangle**'s sides. The perpendicular bisectors of the big triangle concur (at *its* circumcenter), so the altitudes of the small triangle concur too. ∎
There's a beautiful identity tying $H$ to the circumcenter $O$ and centroid $G$:
Equivalently, $H = 3G - 2O$. So $H$, $G$, $O$ are **collinear** for every triangle, with $G$ between $O$ and $H$, dividing $OH$ in ratio $OG : GH = 1 : 2$.
This common line is the **Euler line** of the triangle — covered in the next lesson.

blocks.title: Coordinates of $H$ (when you need them)
blocks.body:
There's no clean averaging formula like centroid's $(A+B+C)/3$. But the Euler relation gives a usable identity:

$H = A + B + C - 2O$

If you know the circumcenter $O$, then $H$ is just $(A + B + C) - 2O$. This works for any triangle.

Alternatively, write down two altitude line equations and solve the system — straightforward but tedious.

md: Right triangle with legs $a = 6$ (vertical) and $b = 8$ (horizontal). Place it with vertices at $A(0, 0)$ (right angle), $B(8, 0)$, $C(0, 6)$.
**The two legs ARE altitudes themselves.** Altitude from $A$ to $BC$ is *not* a leg, but altitudes from $B$ and $C$ to the opposite sides ARE the legs.
**They meet at the right-angle vertex** $A$. So $H = A = (0, 0)$.
**General rule:** in any right triangle, the orthocenter sits exactly at the right-angle vertex. (Two of the altitudes coincide with the two legs.)

blocks.title: Equilateral special case
blocks.body:
For an equilateral triangle, all four classical centers ($I$, $G$, $O$, $H$) coincide. So the orthocenter is just the geometric center.

For an isosceles (non-equilateral) triangle, $H$ lies on the axis of symmetry (the altitude from the apex IS one of the altitudes).

md: ### Cool fact: each vertex is the orthocenter of the 'other' triangle
If $H$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$, then $A$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle HBC$, $B$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle AHC$, and $C$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle ABH$.
Each of these four points plays the role of orthocenter for the triangle made of the other three. This is called an **orthocentric system** — a beautiful symmetry that pops up in olympiad geometry.
